channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.mmorpg.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.mmorpg.com

item.title: Last Flag Review: Capture the Flag ReimaginedÂ
item.content:encoded:
Growing up, I was in the Boy Scouts.  I wasn’t just in it - I was fully immersed. My parents and brothers were all very actively part of the system and so I was in scout meetings every week and out camping at least once a month with a week-long trip to summer camp once a year. Every night around the campfire, we’d always try to figure out what we were gonna do tonight and very often the answer was playing Capture The Flag (CTF).  Even as the least athletic boy scout in the history of the organization, I have a ton of fond memories of planning and executing a flag capture or running wildly through the woods as I realize my team is very close to losing.  Last Flag attempts to scratch a CTF itch that I didn’t even know I had.

Aside from Boy Scouts, I was also a really big fan of playing games like Unreal Tournament and Halo Reach for their CTF modes. With Reach in specific, I can remember many nights threatening to destroy my Xbox 360 or jumping out of my seat and waking everyone at home up with my cries of victory.  So when I was offered the chance to play Last Flag, it was a no-brainer for me.

My initial reaction to seeing the trailer for Last Flag was “Huh… they added a CTF mode to Fortnite.” Looking in the comment section, I realized I was far from the only one who thought that. It’s not a new Fortnite mode though. Last Flag is an Arena Shooter that plays Capture The Flag. It was developed by Night Street Games, a video game studio founded by Imagine Dragons vocalist Dan Reynolds. Reynolds is no stranger to video games having previously performed Legend of Zelda music at The Game Awards. Imagine Dragons also composed “Children of the Sky” for the 2023 Bethesda game, Starfield.  

The vibe in Last Flag is a 1970s game show. Each character is called a contestant and there’s even an announcer that humorously calls each match. As an Arena Shooter, each contestant has their own skill set, weapons, and abilities. As of right now there are 9 playable characters including a stuntman, an assassin, and even a highly trained Forest Scout (Yeah, he’s just a boy scout.)

Each contestant fulfills a different function. Lumberjack is a tank. He’s difficult to play and even more difficult to play against. Arsenal, a genius inventor from Brazil is an area control specialist.  She can set up turrets that help defend against opponents and machines to help her and her allies.  

Because of each contestant’s unique skill set, while Last Flag looked a lot like Fortnite, it played a lot more like Overwatch.  Like Overwatch, one of my biggest problems was that no one works together. In an objective based shooter, each person should know their lane and stick to it but there were times when I was playing in random matches with people who refused to talk to each other.  This caused me to run into people playing scout when their contestant was support. This caused many matches where my team never even found the enemy flag because I honestly think no one was bothering to look for it.

To their credit, Last Flag implemented many ways to communicate. While game chats have largely migrated over to Discord amongst friends, Last Flag understands that you may need to quickly communicate with people you don’t know. Because of that, there’s both text based chat and in-party voice chat. I can remember playing Halo Reach as a young adult and having my ears assaulted by 12 year olds who had just learned a new word but it’s been so long since a game has suddenly had people talking to me, that I was caught off guard the first time a teammate wanted to communicate in Last Flag.

The game in which that happened provided me with the best experience I’ve had so far in Last Flag.  That’s not because we won. It’s because even though not everyone was talking, everyone was listening.  We moved together as a unit. People knew where they were supposed to be. Perhaps most importantly, everyone had the ability to just have fun playing the game instead of spending time upset about what our teammates were or weren’t doing.

One of the most important mechanics in Last Flag that really sets it apart is upgrading abilities in real time.  The host reminds viewers regularly that money won shouldn’t be money saved. The primary and secondary ability for each contestant can be upgraded for $1500. After spending the cash, a notification lets you know how it’s been upgraded.  For instance, when upgrading the secondary ability of my favorite contestant, Skyfire, it’ll say that now when I launch up, there’s fire left behind. Then the next time I upgrade it, it’ll tell me that fire left behind is dealing considerably more damage if it touches an opponent. Scout (yes, that’s his name) can move quickly through the map with his secondary ability but it lasts longer the more it’s upgraded.

This really sets it apart because sometimes whether or not you succeed comes down to whether or not you upgraded your abilities. At the beginning of the match, you go shoot small robots called “cash bots” that look like something right out of Borderlands to earn some quick cash or you hide your team’s flag and earn some cash that way instead. Then, you earn cash for doing things in the game like eliminating opponents, or capturing spawn towers. It makes it feel like you have more control over your success because you’re not stuck in a game play loop where everyone’s stats are set in stone.

There were so many times early on that I was playing that I thought “Gee, this would be a lot more fun if I could just play it as a mode on Fortnite.” I thought it would be better because I’d be able to play as one of the many characters I’ve accrued, I wouldn’t have to learn a whole new system of game physics, and I would be better at the game. This was in fact just a fallacy though. The more I played, the more I improved. The more I improved, the more fun I had.

I wouldn’t worry if you’re not proficient right at first. There’s a training gym and bot battles you can play to hone your skills. The only criticism I have of doing that is that the training gym is entirely too easy and the bot battles are a huge step up in difficulty without a way to adjust that difficulty.  If the bot battles were able to be adjusted in difficulty, it would solve multiple problems.  Currently, there’s not a good way to let kids improve if they’re playing because no mode is quite easy enough for many kids. It would also allow people not as versed in Arena Shooters to improve before getting thrown into a match with someone who may or may not utilize the on-board voice chat to criticize your skill level.

Also, the more I played, the more I fell in love with the contestants even if I wasn’t playing as them. They have so much personality and Last Flag really gets out its own way to let that shine.  More contestants are coming with Graffiti being announced for summer 2026, but for now, leveling up allows you to try new looks by customizing your contestant with different outfits and finishing moves.  I personally like finishing my opponents with a wrestling elbow move.  It makes me feel like I’m in the WWE. You can also earn celebrations and dances for your contestant as well as player icons.

You don’t have to be a big Arena Shooter fan—or have a childhood full of campfire Capture The Flag—to find something to enjoy. If you give Last Flag a little time and meet it on its own terms, there’s a really fun, chaotic rhythm to it that starts to click. It’s weird, it’s loud, it doesn’t always go the way you want it to—but when it works, Last Flag hits in a way that’s hard to replicate anywhere else.  

7.5 Good

Pros

  • Memorable contestants with personality and distinct roles.
  • The game shines when players communicate and Last Flag provides plenty of opportunity for that.
  • The upgrade system adds strategy keeps matches from feeling stale

Cons

  • The training gym is too easy and the bot matches jump in difficulty to sharply with no room for adjustment
  • When communication is poor, the entire experience suffers

Mike BC

Mike BC is in Las Vegas, NV where he is a husband, father, minister, and gamer (in that order). Currently, he plays a lot of Elite: Dangerous Odyssey, Fortnite, Fall Guys, and enjoys games on the Nintendo Switch. You can follow him at CMDRErekSprax on Twitch!



https://www.mmorpg.com/reviews/last-flag-review-capture-the-flag-reimagined-2000137900
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
